structure.rationale: A practice-stand reference, not a course. Order follows how the shapes are learned and used: first the reading conventions (chord boxes, ASCII tab, finger numbers, x/o), then the five open CAGED chords in C-A-G-E-D order, then the same five as movable barre shapes, then the minor-pentatonic boxes in real-world order (Box 1 first — the one everybody plays), then major pentatonic (which is the same five shapes with the root moved), then the blues scale (minor pentatonic + the b5 blue note). Left page of every spread is the playable artifact (diagram + tab); right page is terse teacher notes.
structure.audience: Guitarists who already fret notes and want a keep-it-open reference for CAGED chords and the pentatonic/blues system — advanced-beginner to intermediate.

conventions.stringOrder: Data arrays run low E (6th) to high e (1st). ASCII tab prints high e on top, low E on bottom (standard tab).
conventions.chordBox: Vertical lines = strings, low E on the LEFT, high e on the RIGHT. Horizontal lines = frets; thick top bar = nut. Number in a dot = fretting finger 1=index 2=middle 3=ring 4=pinky. Above the nut: O = play open, x = don't play/mute.
conventions.roots: A filled/marked dot = the root note (names the chord/scale). Blue-note dots (blues scale) marked separately.

claims.text: In a chord box, vertical lines are strings with low E on the left and high e on the right; horizontal lines are frets, and the thick bar at the top is the nut.
A number inside a fingered dot is which finger frets it: 1=index, 2=middle, 3=ring, 4=pinky. Above the nut, O means play the open string and x means mute/don't play it.
Standard ASCII tab writes six lines with high e on top and low E on bottom; a number on a line is the fret to press on that string, 0 is open, x is muted.

pitfalls: Reading the box upside-down (high e on the left) — low E is always the left-most vertical line.
Confusing fret numbers with finger numbers — the box shows frets, the dot label shows the finger.

claims.text: Open C major is x-3-2-0-1-0 (low E muted): A string 3rd fret, D string 2nd fret, G open, B string 1st fret, high e open.
Its C-major root sits on the 5th (A) string, 3rd fret; this is why the movable version keeps its root on the 5th string.

pitfalls: Strumming the low E — start the strum on the A string.
Flat ring finger deadening the open G — arch it.

claims.text: Open A major is x-0-2-2-2-0: A open, D/G/B strings all 2nd fret, high e open, low E not played.
The root is on the 5th (A) string; the three fretted notes crowd one fret so the trick is fitting three fingers into one fret space.

pitfalls: Fingers touching the open high e — keep them arched.
Playing the low E string.

claims.text: Open G major is 3-2-0-0-0-3: low E 3rd fret, A 2nd fret, D/G/B open, high e 3rd fret.
The G shape spreads across all six strings with roots on the 6th and 1st strings; a 4-finger version 3-2-0-0-3-3 adds a fuller top.

pitfalls: The wide reach from low-E to high-e; keep the wrist forward.
Muting the open middle strings with a collapsed hand.

claims.text: Open E major is 0-2-2-1-0-0: low E open, A 2nd fret, D 2nd fret, G 1st fret, B open, high e open.
The root is on the 6th (low E) string; this shape becomes the workhorse 6th-string-root barre chord.

pitfalls: Index leaning off the G string 1st fret onto the open B.
This is the shape to memorise first for barre chords.

claims.text: Open D major is x-x-0-2-3-2 on the top four strings: D open, G 2nd fret, B 3rd fret, high e 2nd fret; low E and A not played.
The root is on the 4th (D) string; the shape is a compact triangle and its movable form stays a top-strings, 4th-string-root shape.

pitfalls: Strumming the A or low E — start on the open D.
Ring finger flattening onto the high e.

claims.text: The open C shape is movable: barre it and the C-form keeps its root on the 5th string. Moved up 2 frets the C shape spells D major, x-5-4-2-3-2.
The full 5-string C-form is an awkward stretch, so it is usually played as a partial (top strings) or used to connect the CAGED shapes rather than as a first-choice barre.

snippets.note: root on 5th string; open C moved up 2 frets

pitfalls: Trying to barre all five strings cleanly — most players take only the top three or four.
The wide finger spread from the root down to the barre.

claims.text: The A-form is a 5th-string-root barre: index barres strings 5-1 at the root fret and the ring finger barres strings 4-3-2 two frets up. At the 2nd fret it spells B major, x-2-4-4-4-2.
It is one of the two everyday barre shapes (with the E-form): its root on the 5th string names the chord.

snippets.note: root on 5th string; index barre at 2, ring barre at 4

pitfalls: The ring-finger barre buzzing on the high e — either let it mute the e or curl the tip.
Playing the low E string.

claims.text: The G-form is a 6th-string-root shape but the full barre is the hardest in CAGED — the reach from the low root to the top notes is huge, so it is almost always played as a partial or a triad on the top strings. Moved up 2 frets from open G it spells A major, 5-4-2-2-2-5.
The G and D shapes are the two prone to spacing errors — keep an empty fret between the index and the upper fingers instead of compressing them.

snippets.note: root on 6th string; usually played as a partial/triad

pitfalls: Compressing the shape — leave the empty fret the pattern needs.
Expecting a clean full barre; use it mostly to connect positions.

claims.text: The E-form is the workhorse 6th-string-root barre: index barres all six strings at the root fret while the E shape sits above. At the 1st fret it spells F major, 1-3-3-2-1-1.
Because its root is on the low E string, sliding the E-form up the neck names any major chord off the 6th string.

snippets.note: root on 6th string; index barres all six at fret 1

pitfalls: Weak index barre — roll it onto its bony side and squeeze from the thumb.
This and the A-form are the two barre shapes to master first.

claims.text: The D-form is a 4th-string-root shape on the top four strings; moved up 2 frets from open D it spells E major, x-x-2-4-5-4.
Like the G-form it is usually a partial/triad shape and one of the two prone to compressing the finger spacing.

pitfalls: Compressing the shape — keep the pattern's fret gaps.
Ringing the A or low E string; keep the strum on the top four.

claims.text: The minor pentatonic scale is 1, b3, 4, 5, b7; A minor pentatonic is A C D E G.

Box 1 of A minor pentatonic sits at the 5th fret: frets per string (low E to high e) are 5-8, 5-7, 5-7, 5-7, 5-8, 5-8, with root A on the 6th string 5th fret, 4th string 7th fret, and 1st string 5th fret.
Box 1 is the most-used shape because its root is on the low E string, so it moves to any key by lining that root up.

pitfalls: Treating it as a closed box — connect it to Box 2 and Box 5 either side.

claims.text: Box 2 of A minor pentatonic (around frets 7-10): 8-10, 7-10, 7-10, 7-9, 8-10, 8-10 (low E to high e), with root A on the 4th string 7th fret and 2nd string 10th fret.
Box 2 connects directly above Box 1 and shares its higher notes; it links the CAGED A-shape region.

pitfalls: Losing the root reference — it is no longer on the low E string here.

claims.text: Box 3 of A minor pentatonic (around frets 9-13): 10-12, 10-12, 10-12, 9-12, 10-13, 10-12 (low E to high e), with root A on the 5th string 12th fret and 2nd string 10th fret.

pitfalls: The stretch on the B string (10 to 13) — use pinky.

claims.text: Box 4 of A minor pentatonic (around frets 12-15): 12-15, 12-15, 12-14, 12-14, 13-15, 12-15 (low E to high e), with root A on the 5th string 12th fret and 4th string 14th fret.
Box 4 overlaps the CAGED E-shape region and leads into Box 5.

pitfalls: At the 12th fret Box 4 is Box 1's shape an octave up in disguise — do not confuse them.

claims.text: Box 5 of A minor pentatonic (around frets 14-17): 15-17, 15-17, 14-17, 14-17, 15-17, 15-17 (low E to high e), with root A on the 6th string 17th fret, 3rd string 14th fret, and 1st string 17th fret.
Box 5 sits just below Box 1's octave and closes the five-shape loop, so Box 5 up here is the same shape as Box 1 lower down.

pitfalls: Do not stop at Box 5 — it wraps back to Box 1 an octave up.

claims.text: The major pentatonic scale is 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 and uses the exact same five shapes as the minor pentatonic — the difference is only where the root sits.

A minor pentatonic and C major pentatonic are the same notes (A C D E G); a minor pentatonic shape becomes its relative major by moving the root focus, or shift the whole shape down 3 frets to keep the same letter name major.

A practical major-pentatonic Box 1: G major pentatonic (G A B D E) at frets 2-5: 3-5, 2-5, 2-5, 2-4, 3-5, 3-5 (low E to high e), with root G on the 6th string 3rd fret, 4th string 5th fret, and 1st string 3rd fret.

snippets.note: same shape as E minor pentatonic Box 1

pitfalls: Playing the shape but resting on the minor root — the major sound needs the major root as home.
Forgetting the 3-fret shift when converting a minor box to the same-letter major.

claims.text: The blues scale is the minor pentatonic with an added b5: 1, b3, 4, b5, 5, b7. A blues scale is A C D Eb E G, the Eb being the blue note.
Easiest learned as minor pentatonic Box 1 plus the blue notes: on A blues, add Eb on the 5th string 6th fret and the 3rd string 8th fret (between D and E).
The b5 is a passing note — you slide or hammer through it into the 4 or 5 rather than sitting on it.

pitfalls: Leaning on the blue note — it is a passing tone, not a landing tone.
Adding the b5 on every string; the two Box-1 spots above are the usable ones.
